subtopics.title: Basic Concepts – CP, SP, Profit, Loss

blocks.title: Cost Price (CP)
blocks.content: The price at which an article is purchased. It includes all expenses incurred to bring the article to the point of sale (overheads).

blocks.title: Selling Price (SP)
blocks.content: The price at which an article is sold. If SP > CP, there is a profit; if SP < CP, there is a loss.

blocks.title: Profit and Loss Formulas
blocks.formula:
Profit = SP – CP
Loss = CP – SP
Profit% = (Profit/CP) × 100
Loss% = (Loss/CP) × 100
blocks.description: Always calculated as percentage of Cost Price.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: CP includes all overheads (transport, packaging, etc.).
Profit and Loss percentages are always based on CP.
If SP is given and profit/loss% is given, we can find CP: CP = SP × 100 / (100 + P%) or CP = SP × 100 / (100 – L%).

blocks.title: Finding CP and SP from Percentages
blocks.subtitle: Reverse Calculations

blocks.title: Example 1: Find CP when SP and Profit% given
Example 2: Find SP when CP and Loss% given
Common Mistake
blocks.wrong: Adding profit% to SP to get CP.
blocks.tip: Always verify by checking: CP + Profit% of CP = SP.

blocks.title: Remember
points: Profit% and Loss% are always on CP.
Use these four formulas extensively.
In exams, questions often ask to find CP or SP given the other and percentage.

blocks.title: Definitions
blocks.content: Marked Price (MP) is the price printed on the item. Discount is a reduction given on MP. Selling Price (SP) = MP – Discount.

blocks.title: Discount Formulas
blocks.formula:
Discount = MP × Discount%
SP = MP × (100 – D%) / 100
MP = SP × 100 / (100 – D%)

blocks.title: Relation between MP, CP, Profit% and Discount%
blocks.content: If there is a profit after discount, then SP = CP × (100+P%)/100 and also SP = MP × (100–D%)/100. Equate the two to find MP or D%.

blocks.title: Example: Find MP when CP, Profit%, Discount% given
blocks.formula_used: Equate SP from CP and MP with discount

blocks.title: Trap: Discount is on MP, not on CP.
blocks.tip: Read the question carefully – it often says 'marked price'.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Discount is always on MP.
SP = MP – Discount.
Use these to solve combined problems.

blocks.title: Concept
blocks.content: When two or more discounts are applied successively, they are not simply added. The overall discount is less than the sum of individual discounts.

blocks.title: Successive Discount Formula
blocks.description: For two discounts D₁% and D₂%. For more, multiply (100 – D%) successively and divide by 100^(n-1).

blocks.title: Example: Find single equivalent discount for 10% and 20% successive discounts.
Example: Calculate SP after two successive discounts.
Common Mistake
blocks.tip: Always use the multiplication formula for successive discounts.

rows.right: More complex but common in exams

blocks.title: Remember
points: Successive discounts are not additive.
Equivalent discount % = 100 – (product of (100 – D%) / 100).
Practice with multiple discounts to avoid traps.

blocks.title: Concept
blocks.content: A dishonest shopkeeper uses a weight that is not true (e.g., 900g instead of 1kg). This creates profit even without marking up the price.

blocks.title: Profit% due to false weight
blocks.formula: Profit% = [ (True Weight – False Weight) / False Weight ] × 100
blocks.description: If he sells at CP per unit weight, profit comes from the weight difference.

blocks.title: Example: Shopkeeper uses 900g instead of 1kg, sells at CP.
Combined Profit with Price Markup
blocks.content: If he also marks up the price, overall profit% = [ (SP per unit / CP per unit) × (True Weight / False Weight) – 1 ] × 100.

blocks.title: Example: Shopkeeper marks up 20% and uses 900g.
steps: Let CP per kg = 100. MP = 120. He gives 900g.
blocks.formula_used: Profit = (MP × True Wt / False Wt) – CP

blocks.title: Trap: Profit% calculation
blocks.wrong: Using profit as (True Wt – False Wt) / True Wt × 100.
blocks.correct: Denominator is False Weight (what he actually paid for).
blocks.tip: Always divide by the cost he incurred (the false weight).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: False weights create profit at no extra price.
Profit% = (True – False) / False × 100 when selling at CP.
If markup is included, combine: SP per unit ÷ (CP per unit × (False Wt / True Wt)).
Practice with different numbers.

subtopics.title: Advanced Relations – CP/SP with Multiple Items

blocks.title: Advanced Problems
blocks.subtitle: Selling Two Items with Same SP or Same CP

blocks.title: Common Pattern
blocks.content: Two items sold at the same SP – one at profit x%, other at loss x%. Then overall loss% = (x²/100)%.

blocks.title: Example: Two items sold at ₹1000 each. One at 20% profit, other at 20% loss. Find overall profit/loss%.
blocks.formula_used: Loss% = (x²/100)% when same SP and same x% profit/loss.

blocks.title: Another Pattern: Same CP, different SP
blocks.content: If an item is sold at two different prices with different profit/loss percentages, we can find CP by equating.

blocks.title: Example: An article sold at ₹600 gives 20% profit. At what price should it be sold to get 30% profit?
blocks.formula_used: Find CP first, then SP for new profit%.

blocks.title: Quick Result Table
rows: Two items same SP, P% & L% same
Two items same CP, different profit%
Overall profit% = average of % if quantities equal
One at P%, one at L%, same quantity

blocks.title: Remember
points: For same SP with same profit and loss %, net loss = x²/100 %.
For same CP, profit/loss can be averaged if quantities same.
Always find CP first when given SP and profit%.

blocks.title: Shortcut 1 – CP from SP and Profit%
Shortcut 2 – CP from SP and Loss%
Shortcut 3 – Equivalent Discount
blocks.content: For two discounts D₁ and D₂, equivalent discount = D₁ + D₂ – (D₁×D₂)/100.

blocks.title: Trap: Discount formula
blocks.tip: Always subtract the product divided by 100.

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Profit = SP – CP; CP is base for percentage; Discount = MP – SP.

blocks.title: Shortcut Summary
points: Equivalent discount for two = D₁ + D₂ – (D₁D₂)/100.
For same SP with equal P & L%, loss% = (x²/100)%.
For false weight profit% = (True – False)/False × 100.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Identify the type: basic, discount, successive, false weight, or advanced relations. Apply the correct shortcut.

blocks.question: A shopkeeper buys an article for ₹400 and sells it for ₹500. The profit percentage is:
If an article is sold at ₹1800 with a profit of 20%, find the CP.
A trader marks his goods 40% above CP and allows a discount of 20%. His profit% is:
Two successive discounts of 20% and 10% are equivalent to a single discount of:
A dishonest shopkeeper uses 800g instead of 1kg and sells at CP. His profit% is:
Two items sold at ₹1000 each. One at 10% profit, other at 10% loss. Overall loss% is:
If CP of an article is ₹500 and SP is ₹650, what is the profit%?
Find the single discount equivalent to 30% and 20% successive discounts.
A shopkeeper marks his goods 25% above CP and gives a discount of 10%. His profit% is:

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review each mistake. Focus on formulas for discounts, false weight, and combined scenarios. Practice more similar questions.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Profit & Loss
points: Profit = SP – CP; Loss = CP – SP.
Profit% and Loss% are on CP.
Discount is on MP.
Successive discount formula: D₁ + D₂ – (D₁D₂)/100.
Same SP with equal P% & L% → Loss = (x²/100)%.
False weight profit% = (True – False)/False × 100.
Use shortcuts to save time.
